coredata.dc:title: Reversible polymerization of carbon dots based on dynamic covalent imine bond
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Bin-Bin
Chang, Shuai
Jiang, Lei
Lv, Jian
Gao, Ya-Ting
Wang, Yue
Qian, Ruo-Can
Li, Da-Wei
Hafez, Mahmoud Elsayed
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as new type of carbon-based nanoparticles, are considered to be an aggregate with irreversible polymerization. Achieving the reversible tunability of CDs luminescence based on their reversible polymerization is a challenging subject. Herein, we, for the first time, design and construct the blue-emitting CDs with reversible polymerization by a room-temperature Schiff base reaction between tannic acid and ethylenediamine. The formation of CDs is proven to be due to the crosslinking polymerization of precursors caused by imine bond. As a dynamic covalent bond, imine bond endows CDs with controllable structural transformation properties, and the prepared CDs can be depolymerized and polymerized reversibly by pH-controlled imine bond cleavage and re-formation. These properties of reversible fluorescence photoswitching make the CDs have a good application prospect in reversible information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dynamic covalent imine bond
Reversible polymerization
Reversible photoswitching
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of acid/alkali on the FL of the CDs
Preparation and characterization of the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation and characterization of the prepared CDs. (a) Schematic illustration for the formation process of CDs. (b) TEM image (inset: size distribution of CDs). The areas marked with white circles are amorphous CDs. (c) High-resolution TEM image, (d) XPS spectrum, high-resolution (e) N1s spectrum, (f) C1s spectrum, and (g) O1s spectrum of CDs. (h) FT-IR spectra of oxidized TA and CDs.
Optical properties of CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths, (c) 3D FL spectra, and (d) relative quantum yield of CDs.

Reversible polymerization of CDs by regulating solution pH. (a) The FL intensity of CDs at different pH. (b) Time-resolved decay of CDs in different concentrations of acid solution. (c) Absorption spectra and (d) Time-resolved decay of CDs in different concentrations of alkali solution. (e) Acid/alkali-controlled reversible FL change of CDs. (f) Reversible absorption spectra of CDs when sequently adding NaOH solution (1 M) and HCl solution (1 M) for five cycles. (g) Hydrodynamic size of CDs when sequently adding NaOH solution (1 M) and HCl solution (1 M) for five cycles. (h) Schematic illustration of the reversible polymerization of CDs by pH-controlled imine bond cleavage and re-formation.

Reversible information encryption of CDs. (a) The designed information encryption pattern with the prepared CDs and Tb-CDs. (b) Schematic illustration of information encryption with the prepared CDs and Tb-CDs. (c) Illustrated luminescent photo-switchable behavior of the prepared CDs and Tb-CDs after adding NaOH and HCl solution.

head.ce:title: Reversible polymerization of carbon dots based on dynamic covalent imine bond

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Beni-Suef University, Beni-Suef 62511, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Beni-Suef University, Beni-Suef 62511, Egypt

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we construct the blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with reversible polymerization by pH-controlled imine bond cleavage and re-formation.

Carbon dots (CDs), as new type of carbon-based nanoparticles, are considered to be an aggregate with irreversible polymerization. Achieving the reversible tunability of CDs luminescence based on their reversible polymerization is a challenging subject. Herein, we, for the first time, design and construct the blue-emitting CDs with reversible polymerization by a room-temperature Schiff base reaction between tannic acid and ethylenediamine. The formation of CDs is proven to be due to the crosslinking polymerization of precursors caused by imine bond. As a dynamic covalent bond, imine bond endows CDs with controllable structural transformation properties, and the prepared CDs can be depolymerized and polymerized reversibly by pH-controlled imine bond cleavage and re-formation. These properties of reversible fluorescence photoswitching make the CDs have a good application prospect in reversible information encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been regarded as the promising zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon-based nanoparticles due to their superior properties such as excellent optical properties, resistance to photobleaching, good biocompatibility, high water solubility, and photostability. Accordingly, CDs have been explored for variety of applications like cellular imaging [1,2], drug delivery [3], photocatalysis [4,5], optoelectronic conversion [6], chem/bio-sensing [7], supercapacitors [8,9], and cancer treatment [10,11], providing a research hotspot in the field of luminescent materials in the recent years.

In current studies, the synthesis of CDs can be divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods [12]. The “bottom-up” formation of CDs usually is considered to be originated from the polymerization and carbonization of small molecular precursors by covalent, non-covalent or other interactions [13–15]. CDs as a nano aggregate are structurally irreversible, that is, once CDs are polymerized from small molecules precursors, it is difficult to control the reversible polymerization and depolymerization of CDs [16]. This may be mainly because the connection mode of small molecular precursors for the polymerization of CDs is a stable and irreversible interaction. To date, there are no any reports, for the best of our knowledge, about the reversible regulation of the degree of polymerization of CDs. The irreversible structural characteristics pose a great challenge to the study of the internal relationship between the structure and optical properties of luminescent CDs, and make it difficult for traditional CDs to be developed into intelligent optical nanomaterials with stimulus–response [17]. Therefore, achieving the reversible regulation of CDs structure is of great significance not only for understanding the fluorescence (FL) mechanism, but also for the development of smart materials with stimulus–response.

Dynamic covalent bonds dissociation and re-formation through a reversible reaction under defined conditions are conducive to the construction of stimulus responsive materials such as polymer networks, hydrogels and supramolecular polymers [18–21]. Common dynamic covalent bonds such as BO bond, SS bond, SeSe bond and SeRu bond have been extensively used in stimuli-responsive materials to achieve self-healing, stress relaxation, shape memory, recyclability [22]. Among them, imine (CN) bond is a typical dynamic covalent bond which is formed by an Schiff-base condensation reaction between an amino group and an aldehyde group [19]. Besides, the pH sensitivity of imine bond allows the dynamic reversible reaction by adjusting the solution pH [23,24]. The incorporation of dynamic covalent bond in CDs is expected to achieve the reversible polymerization of CDs.

Herein, we design a platform to synthesize blue-emitting CDs by one-pot Schiff base reaction using tannic acid (TA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) precursors. CDs are formed by the imine bond crosslinking between the precursor molecules. We can further control the imine bond cleavage and re-formation in CDs constructed by dynamic covalent imine bond by controlling the solution pH since the obtained CDs are pH-responsive, leading to a cycle on/off photoswitching based on the reversible polymerization of CDs. The proposed reversible polymerization mechanism sheds light on the potential use of CDs for information encryption.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and TA were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). EDA was obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Dialysis membrane for purification of CDs was commercially available from Solarbio Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). All solutions were prepared using 18.2 MΩ.cm ultrapure water (Millipore-Q, TONDINO, Shanghai).

The morphology of CDs was measured with a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). The element composition of CDs was obtained from a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs was performed on a Hitachi FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). A USB 2000 + spectrometer (Ocean Optics Inc., USA) was used for UV–vis spectrum of CDs. The FL spectra were collected from F97pro FL spectrophotometer (Lengguang, Shanghai). The hydrodynamic sizes of CDs were measured using a ZEN3600 dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, English).

CDs were prepared at room temperature. In detail, 50 mg TA (29.4 μmol) was dissolved in 4.8 mL water and then 0.2 mL EDA (3 mmol) was added into the solution. The obtained solution mixture was placed under atmosphere environment for 3 days. Thereafter, the product was subjected to dialysis process using a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (100–500 MWCO). Finally, the resulting products were dried by lyophilization to acquire CDs, then dispersed in water for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of acid/alkali on the FL of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of acid/alkali on the FL of CDs was tested as follows: 0.2 mL different concentrations of HCl (10 μM–10 M) or NaOH (10 μM–10 M) and 0.02 mL CDs solution (1 mg/mL) were added to 1.78 mL water in turn. The obtained mixture was incubated for 5 min before testing the FL intensity. All FL spectra were measured at maximum excitation wavelength of 330 nm. For pH-controlled reversible polymerization experiment of CDs, 20 μL CDs solution (1 mg/mL) was mixed with 1980 μL water, then 20 μL NaOH solution (10 M) and 20 μL HCl solution (10 M) were added into CDs solution in turn and recycled for 5 times. The obtained mixture is used to test the FL intensity and absorption spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Element composition of CDs has been investigated by the XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 d, CDs are consisted of carbon (284.9 eV), nitrogen (399.1 eV), and oxygen (530.7 eV) atoms with atomic percentages of 64.45%, 20.52%, and 15.04%, respectively (Table S1). The high-resolution XPS spectra further reveal that CDs have abundant CC, CN, CO, and OH bonds ( Fig. 1 e–g).[28] This result is also confirmed by the FT-IR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1 h, FT-IR spectrum of oxidized TA shows the characteristic absorption bands of OH, CO, and CC stretching vibrations at 3457, 1723 and 1633 cm−1, respectively. After the formation of CDs, CDs mainly contain OH/NH (3355 cm−1), CN (1649 cm−1), CN (1384 cm−1), CH (1328 cm−1), and C-OH (1157 cm−1) bonds [29–31]. The disappearance of CO bond and the appearance of CN structure in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs strongly suggest that the production of CDs is due to the crosslinking polymerization of precursors caused by the dynamic covalent imine bond.

UV–vis spectrum of CDs shows two absorption bands at about 260 and 330 nm ( Fig. 2
a), which are attributed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic ring structure and the n-π* transition of the CN bond [32,33]. FL spectrum of CDs shows that the maximum emission wavelength at about 406 nm can be obtained at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm ( Fig. 2 b). Meanwhile, the prepared CDs have an excitation-independent fluorescent emission under the excitation wavelength range of 300–360 nm ( Fig. 2 c). Moreover, the relative quantum yield (Quinine sulfate as a reference) of CDs is about 2.67% ( Fig. 2 d), with a bright blue FL emission under UV light. This luminescent efficiency is significantly lower than that of CDs obtained by high-temperature carbonization, but obviously higher than that of other CDs synthesized at room temperature (Table S2). Although the luminescent efficiency of the prepared CDs is low, the facile preparation (one-pot synthesis) and low cost (room-temperature synthesis) make CDs have great potential in the application of information encryption.

Importantly, the FL intensity of CDs can be reversibly regulated because of the dynamic tunability of imine bond. As shown in Fig. 3 e, CDs are firstly depolymerized in a high concentration of NaOH solution (1 M) and the FL of CDs decreases significantly. Interestingly, CDs polymerize again after neutralization with HCl solution (1 M), and the FL of CDs returns to the original intensity. The “on–off–on” cycles of FL intensity indicates that the depolymerization and polymerization process of CDs is reversible. The reversible polymerization absorption spectra clearly reveal the pH-controlled reversible dynamic covalent imine bond cleavage and re-formation ( Fig. 3 f). After adding NaOH solution, the depolymerization of CDs happens that red-shifts the characteristic absorption peak of CDs from 260 nm to 279 nm which is corresponded to oxidized TA. Fascinatingly, the original absorption peak at 260 nm is recovered after the neutralization with HCl solution.

Based on pH-controlled reversible polymerization properties, we develop a simple anti-counterfeiting strategy for reversible information encryption. Terbium-doped CDs (Tb-CDs) are used to design code numbers because of their stable blue FL in strong alkali solution ( Fig. 4
a) [37]. As shown in Fig. 4 b, NaOH solution (1 M) is firstly mixed with the prepared CDs and Tb-CDs (0.1 μg/mL, low concentration). The blue FL of CDs is quenched, meanwhile, Tb-CDs are also hardly luminescent because of their low concentration. In view of this, the encrypted information under 365 nm UV light irradiation is invisible. However, the blue FL of CDs will be recovered and the FL of Tb-CDs will not change after the solution environment becomes neutral by adding HCl solution (1 M). Therefore, a blue “229” pattern appears.

Furthermore, we design another code number for reversible information encryption ( Fig. 4 c). The FL intensity of Tb-CDs (1 mg/mL, high concentration) is first adjusted to be consistent with that of the prepared CDs. Upon 365 nm UV light irradiation, a blue “888” pattern is visible. After adding the NaOH solution (1 M), the “888” pattern is decoded into a “211” because the FL of the CDs can be quenched and the FL of Tb-CDs is not changed in strong alkali solution. Further acid neutralization causes the FL recovery of the CDs, the blue “211” pattern is transformed into the “888” pattern. Therefore, the obtained CDs show a good application prospect in reversible information encryption.

In conclusion, we develop a unique CDs with a cycle “on–off” photoswitching by a room-temperature Schiff base reaction. The formation of CDs is due to the crosslinking polymerization caused by imine bond. The incorporation of dynamic covalent imine bond in CDs makes the polymerization of CDs dynamically reversible. Because of the reversible polymerization property, CDs as reversibly regulated fluorescent materials are well used to information encryption, indicating great promising for the development of smart responsive materials.

Bin-Bin Chen: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shuai Chang: Visualization, Investigation. Lei Jiang: Investigation. Jian Lv: Investigation. Ya-Ting Gao: . Yue Wang: Investigation. Ruo-Can Qian: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Da-Wei Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Mahmoud Elsayed Hafez: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21788102, 21777041, 21974046, 22176058, and 21977031), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (19391901700, 19520744000, and 19ZR1472300), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.04.089.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
